channel.title: NYT > World News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Iran War Live Updates: Israel Appears to Pull Back From Threat to Strike Beirut
item.description: President Trump is pressing Israel not to attack the Lebanese capital. An escalation in Israel’s campaign against Hezbollah could threaten peace talks with Iran.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: How Lebanon’s Best Chance to Disarm Hezbollah Failed
item.description: Lebanon’s government has long wanted the powerful militia to give up its weapons. Before the Iran war began, there were signs of progress toward that goal.
item.dc:creator: Abdi Latif Dahir and Aaron Boxerman
item.category: Arms Control and Limitation and Disarmament
item.media:description: Climbing through rubble in central Beirut, Lebanon, after an Israeli airstrike in March.

item.title: Russia Launches Deadly Strikes on Ukraine
item.description: Missiles and drones struck the cities of Kyiv and Dnipro, Ukrainian officials said. At least 10 people were killed.
item.dc:creator: Maria Varenikova
item.media:description: An explosion in Kyiv, Ukraine, during a Russian strike on Tuesday morning.

item.title: The New Zealand Parakeet Pair That Are Saving Their Species
item.description: There are about 450 orange-fronted parakeets left in the world. Super breeders Nacho and Trixie are doing their best to bolster the population.
item.dc:creator: Laura Chung
item.media:credit: Leigh Percasky/The Isaac Conservation and Wildlife Trust, via AFP — Getty Images

item.title: An Industrial Gem in Venezuela Now Embodies the Country’s Decay
item.description: Cumaná was once an economic hub, producing Toyota Land Cruisers and exporting food across South America. Now it’s on the brink of disaster as public services collapse.
item.dc:creator: Simon Romero and Adriana Loureiro Fernandez
item.media:description: Gathering water at a stream in Cumaná.

item.title: Alberta’s Movement to Separate from Canada Gets Its Moment
item.description: In October, Albertans will get to say if they want to stay in Canada, or hold a referendum to leave. Will it settle the matter, or deepen the rift?
item.dc:creator: Matina Stevis-Gridneff and Amber Bracken
item.media:description: A table set up to gather signatures for a separation petition along a road in Calgary, Alberta, in February.

item.title: UK Releases More Files About Mandelson, Ex-Diplomat With Ties to Epstein
item.description: Peter Mandelson, who was fired over his links to Jeffrey Epstein, made the claim in a letter among more than a thousand pages of documents released on Monday.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle and Megan Specia
item.media:description: Peter Mandelson, a former British ambassador to the United States, in London in April. He was fired from the post in September over his links to the convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.

item.title: Andriy Yermak, Once the Muscle Behind Zelensky, Is Now Crowdfunding Bail Money
item.description: Volodymyr Zelensky’s onetime right-hand man is accused of embezzling millions of dollars and consulting a fortuneteller on political decisions.
item.dc:creator: Siobhán O’Grady
item.media:description: Andriy Yermak, who was chief of staff to President Volodymyr Zelensky until November, in court last month in Kyiv, Ukraine.

item.title: Syrian Officers Who Hid in Plain Sight Face War Crime Charges in Austria
item.description: The trial, Austria’s first against Assad regime officials, will be a rare chance for Syrian witnesses to confront two men they have accused of torture.
item.dc:creator: Carlotta Gall
item.category: War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
Commission for International Justice and Accountability
item.media:description: Khaled al-Halabi, a Syrian former brigadier general, entering court in Vienna on Monday. He is the highest-ranking official of the Assad regime to appear in a European court on charges of war crimes.

item.title: Britain Denies Entry to Hasan Piker and Cenk Uygur
item.description: The authorities did not say why they had barred Cenk Uygur and Hasan Piker, who have popular social media channels, but said their presence “may not be conducive to the public good.”
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg
item.media:description: Cenk Uygur in New York in 2023. He called the decision to cancel his travel authorization “absolutely Kafkaesque.”

item.title: Ethiopia’s Election Comes at a Critical Moment for the Country: What to Know
item.description: The vote comes at a critical moment for the country, which faces fears of renewed conflict in the Tigray region and tensions with Eritrea over access to the Red Sea.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Mpoke Bigg
item.media:description: Outside a polling station in Addis Ababa, Ethiopia’s capital, on Monday.

item.title: Dozens Killed as Explosion Flattens Rebel-Held Village in Myanmar
item.description: The local authorities said the cause was an accident at a warehouse that held explosives for mining operations.
item.dc:creator: Sui-Lee Wee
item.media:description: The aftermath of an explosion on Sunday in Kaung Tup, a village in Myanmar, as seen in a handout photo provided by Palaung Land, a group with links to the rebel group that controls the area.

item.title: Brooklyn Rivera, Nicaraguan Indigenous Leader, Dies in Government Custody
item.description: Brooklyn Rivera, 73, one of the most prominent Miskito political leaders in recent history, was arrested by the government in 2023. His family and other groups dispute the circumstances surrounding his death.
item.dc:creator: James Wagner
item.media:description: The Miskito rebel leader Brooklyn Rivera, right, speaking during a news conference in Managua, Nicaragua, as his fellow leader Steadman Fagoth Müller looked on, in 1988.

item.title: Taiwan Criticizes China’s Expulsion of New York Times Reporter
item.description: The Taiwan president’s spokeswoman said China used “baseless pretexts” to punish journalists, which she called evidence that Beijing was a “troublemaker.”
item.dc:creator: David Pierson
item.media:description: Taiwan’s president, Lai Ching-te, in Taipei last month.

item.title: Trump Targets Brazil With 25% Tariff, Citing Unfair Trade Practices
item.description: The proposal marks the latest effort by the administration to rebuild its tariff agenda through Section 301 investigations.
item.dc:creator: Daisuke Wakabayashi
item.media:description: Bulk carriers at the port of Paranagua, Brazil, in March.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman, Christina Goldbaum, Farnaz Fassihi and Hari Raj

item.title: Security Council Members Call for Israel to Withdraw From Southern Lebanon
item.description: The U.N. panel met after Israel had threatened to attack southern Beirut. President Trump said later that Israel and Hezbollah had agreed not to attack each other.
item.dc:creator: Farnaz Fassihi
item.media:description: Fleeing the southern suburbs of Beirut, a family sheltered on the roadside Monday amid growing traffic, after Israel warned the area would be targeted.

item.title: As Ebola Spreads, Scientists Race to Find Vaccines and Treatments
item.description: The Bundibugyo virus, a little known type, previously had caused just two small outbreaks. Now it’s at the center of a rapidly widening epidemic in Africa.
item.dc:creator: Carl Zimmer
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Health workers disinfected a coffin containing the body of a man presumed have died of Ebola in the Democratic Republic of Congo.

item.title: Crested Ibises Get the Royal Treatment in Japan as They Fly Again
item.description: Once widespread in Japan, the colorful birds went from being fairly commonplace in the country to being on the verge of extinction.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni
item.media:description: Two crested ibises were released into the wild during a ceremony attended by Japan’s crown prince and princess on Sunday.

item.title: Trump Stands to Gain a Key Ally in Colombia’s Upcoming Election
item.description: Abelardo De La Espriella, a hard-line candidate on the right facing a left-wing rival in a presidential runoff, has pledged to crush drug traffickers.
item.dc:creator: Annie Correal and Luis Ferré-Sadurní
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Abelardo De La Espriella, at a campaign event in Medellín in May, captured more than 40 percent of the vote on Sunday.

item.title: Trump Finds High-Wire Iran Negotiations ‘Very Boring’
item.description: President Trump told CNBC that he “couldn’t care less” if the negotiations with Iran break down.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump at the White House in Washington in May.

item.title: Mette Frederiksen Forms New Government in Denmark
item.description: Mette Frederiksen may not be nearly as popular as she once was, but she remains the Danes’ most dominant leader in decades.
item.dc:creator: Jeffrey Gettleman, Amelia Nierenberg and Maya Tekeli
item.media:credit: Sebastian Elias Uth/Ritzau Scanpix Denmark, via Reuters
item.media:description: Prime Minister Mette Frederiksen of Denmark speaking to reporters in front of the Royal Yacht Dannebrog on Monday.

item.title: Trump Says Israel and Hezbollah Will Hold Off on New Military Action
item.description: A statement from Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu of Israel did not mention a new cease-fire, but the Lebanese government said one was taking shape.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman, Christina Goldbaum, Johnatan Reiss and Richard Pérez-Peña
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: A pro-government demonstration in Tehran on Saturday. Despite a cease-fire announced between Iran and the United States in early April, both countries have continued to trade strikes.

item.title: Inside the Ebola Outbreak
item.description: A Times correspondent is reporting from the Ebola epicenter in the Democratic Republic of Congo. Here’s what he saw.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times

item.title: Hezbollah Ally Says the Iran-Backed Group Wants a New Truce With Israel
item.description: In an interview with The Times, Nabih Berri, an influential Lebanese lawmaker close to Hezbollah, called on President Trump to broker a new cease-fire.
item.dc:creator: Christina Goldbaum
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Workers at the scene of a strike in Choueifat, Lebanon, south of Beirut, on Thursday.

item.title: Dua Lipa and Callum Turner Get Married at London’s Old Marylebone Town Hall
item.description: The Old Marylebone Town Hall, among the busiest wedding venues in Britain, has hosted nuptials for A-listers, including Sylvester Stallone, Paul McCartney and Lena Dunham.
item.dc:creator: Leo Sands
item.media:description: Callum Turner and Dua Lipa were married at the Old Marylebone Town Hall in London on Sunday.

item.title: Netanyahu Orders Israeli Military to Attack Beirut Suburbs
item.description: Thousands fled the southern suburbs of Lebanon’s capital after Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu of Israel announced the widening of the military campaign against Hezbollah.
item.dc:creator: Christina Goldbaum and Aaron Boxerman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Traffic as people fled the southern suburbs in Beirut on Monday.

item.title: Chinese Military Sought Nvidia Chips for Years, Report Says
item.description: An analysis of six years of procurement records suggests that the People’s Liberation Army has openly tried to acquire restricted U.S. technology.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson and Tripp Mickle
item.media:description: China’s military has stepped up its efforts to acquire artificial intelligence chips despite U.S. restrictions, according to Wirescreen, a software platform.

item.title: China Aims A.I. at Predicting Who Could Pose a Political Risk
item.description: New research examines how a Chinese company struggled to develop its predictive surveillance technology while U.S. restrictions were in place.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes
item.media:credit: Lam Yik Fei for The New York Times
item.media:description: The skyline of Beijing. A Chinese firm is working on products that use A.I. to examine location data and internet use to predict who could do or say something critical of the government.

item.title: What We Saw Driving Through Venezuela
item.description: Our reporter Simon Romero takes a roadtrip from Caracas to Cumaná in Venezuela, showing the multiple military checkpoints the regime has along the coast, a constant reminder of the state’s presence.
item.dc:creator: Simon Romero, Leila Medina, Thomas Vollkommer, Joey Sendaydiego and Adriana Loureiro Fernandez

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman, Christina Goldbaum, Johnatan Reiss, Richard Pérez-Peña and Farnaz Fassihi

item.title: U.S. Says It Hit More Military Targets in Southern Iran
item.description: The U.S. military said it had carried out strikes over the weekend and had intercepted an Iranian missile attack on American forces in Kuwait.
item.dc:creator: Yan Zhuang
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Fishing boats idle along the Strait of Hormuz at Qeshm Island, Iran, in May.

item.title: 2025 Wildfires Were the Costliest Ever, Researchers Say
item.description: Severe, hard-to-control blazes in densely populated areas like Los Angeles drove the year’s record losses.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Dzombak
item.media:credit: Loren Elliott for The New York Times
item.media:description: Pacific Palisades, Calif., on Jan. 7, 2025. Fires in the Los Angeles area last year burned at least 90 square miles, killing at least 31 people and displacing more than 150,000.

item.title: China Exports Surveillance
item.description: The country has spent decades perfecting a surveillance state at home. Now it’s promoting its ideology of state control, and the technology to enforce it, abroad.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.media:credit: Adam Ferguson for The New York Times

item.title: Timeline: Where and When Trump Has Involved the U.S. Military in International Conflicts
item.description: The United States has been involved in many military operations around the world since President Trump returned to office.
item.dc:creator: Helene Cooper
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
US Strikes on Alleged Drug Traffickers (2025- )
US Strikes on ISIS in Nigeria (2025- )
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Members of the military transferring the remains of two Iowa National Guard members killed in Syria at Dover Air Force Base in Delaware last year.

item.title: Israel Captures Crusader Castle That Symbolized Its Long Lebanon Occupation
item.description: The seizure of Beaufort in southern Lebanon called up bitter memories in both countries amid a widening Israeli conflict with Hezbollah that seems far from over.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman and Natan Odenheimer
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A view of the Beaufort Castle fortification atop a cliff above the Litani River in southern Lebanon, last year.

item.title: Colombia Presidential Election Heads to a Runoff
item.description: The candidate, Abelardo de la Espriella, will face a senator from the left-wing party of the departing president, Gustavo Petro, in a June runoff.
item.dc:creator: Annie Correal, Genevieve Glatsky and Luis Ferré-Sadurní
item.media:description: Presidential candidate Abelardo De La Espriella addressing supporters in Barranquilla, Colombia, on Sunday.

item.title: Even Havana’s Street Sweepers Can’t Escape the Impact of the Fuel Blockade
item.description: A fuel blockade on Cuba has aggravated a lack of consistent garbage pickup and produced enormous trash piles.
item.dc:creator: Ed Augustin and Frances Robles
item.media:description: Garbage on the street in Havana in December.

item.title: To Improve Ukraine’s Military, She Calls Out the Ugly Parts
item.description: Olha Reshetylova has been tasked by the government with overseeing soldiers’ rights. Some commanders resent her, but she says her work makes the armed forces stronger.
item.dc:creator: Maria Varenikova
item.media:credit: Oksana Parafeniuk for The New York Times
item.media:description: Olha Reshetylova, Ukraine’s first military ombudsman, last month at a training center in western Ukraine. “Even under shelling, even in the army, even during war — first and foremost is dignity,” she said.

item.title: India’s Hindu Right Has a New Hero: A 17th-Century Warrior King
item.description: Statues of Shivaji are rising everywhere. The founder of the Maratha empire who fought against the Mughal dynasty is now a symbol for nationalists remaking the country.
item.dc:creator: Anupreeta Das, Suhasini Raj and Atul Loke
item.media:description: Visitors posing for selfies at the Shivaji statue in Malvan, a small town in Maharashtra, India, in January. There has been a surge of interest in the Hindu warrior in recent years.

item.title: The Sculptor Riding India’s Shivaji Craze
item.description: Demand has soared for his figurines and statues of the warrior king. It started with a video he posted online.
item.dc:creator: Anupreeta Das, Suhasini Raj and Atul Loke
item.media:description: Anant Ganpat Chougule working on a statue of the Maratha king Shivaji at his studio in Sawantwadi, a small town in Maharashtra, India, in January.

item.title: Joby Demonstrated its Air Taxi in Manhattan, but You Can’t Fly in It Yet
item.description: Aviation start-ups and the Trump administration want to replace helicopters with electric aircraft, but the new vehicles still have to pass arduous tests before the public can use them.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi

item.title: As Deaths From U.S. Boat Strikes Pass 200, Locals Tally an Even Greater Cost
item.description: Residents of coastal communities in Colombia and Ecuador said the airstrike campaign was making many reconsider anything involving the ocean as a livelihood.
item.dc:creator: Max Bearak and José María León Cabrera
item.category: US Strikes on Alleged Drug Traffickers (2025- )
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: The remains of a burned boat near Puerto López, Guajira, Colombia in December.

item.title: Where Is Toronto’s Best Party? Try the Basement.
item.description: Millennials who yearn for a bygone era of dancing that feels pure and more organic are bringing back the basement jam, popularized decades ago by Toronto’s Caribbean diaspora.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai and Ian Willms
item.media:description: Unlike most nightclubs, the Caribbean-style basement jams don’t adhere to strict dress-codes, and there are no private booths. The parties hark back to a simpler time.

item.title: In Taiwan, ‘Mainland Spouses’ From China Become a Focus of Infiltration Fears
item.description: At the heart of a criminal case involving one such wife is a question: How can Taiwan protect its democracy without unfairly targeting Chinese migrants?
item.dc:creator: Chris Buckley and Amy Chang Chien
item.media:credit: Lam Yik Fei for The New York Times
item.media:description: A village near Taipei. Taiwan officials are confronting what they say is growing Chinese infiltration of the island’s politics, media and internet.

item.title: Edgar Morin, ‘Grandfather’ of French Intellectuals, Dies at 104
item.description: A former member of the Resistance, he went on to a career spanning eras and disciplines. His books and pronouncements carried moral authority.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nossiter
item.media:description: Edgar Morin in 2003. “I have a permanent sense of the mystery of all things,” he said. “I still have no idea why I was born, why I exist.”

item.title: Iran’s Team Trains in Limbo for World Cup Overshadowed by War
item.description: The Iranian soccer players, still awaiting visas to the U.S., are practicing in Turkey and making backup plans.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Members of Iran’s national soccer team practicing in Antalya, Turkey, on Thursday.

item.title: A Big Bang, a Fire and Panic as War Enters Romanian Homes
item.description: When a drone crashed into an apartment building in eastern Romania, residents were reminded that the Russia-Ukraine war makes for a dangerous neighbor.
item.dc:creator: Andreea Campeanu and Andrew Higgins
item.media:credit: Andreea Campeanu for The New York Times
item.media:description: A temporary cover for a hole in the roof of a building in Galati, Romania, on Saturday, after a Russian drone slammed into a residential block on Friday.

item.title: What I Saw Inside an Ebola Ward
item.description: Our chief Africa correspondent, Declan Walsh, walked inside an Ebola ward where both adults and children were being treated, and where someone had recently died of the disease amid a spiraling outbreak in the region. This is what he saw.
item.dc:creator: Declan Walsh, Estelle Caswell, Thomas Vollkommer and Arlette Bashizi

item.title: As Trump Mulls Decision About Iran War Deal, a Restive Middle East Waits to Hear
item.description: The president has wavered on whether to move ahead with an agreement with Iran to end the war. On Friday, he vowed to make a “final determination” soon.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman and Leily Nikounazar
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: President Trump said on Friday that he was meeting with advisers to make a “final determination” about a proposed deal with Iran.

item.title: Ye and Travis Scott Concerts Canceled in Italy Over Security Fears
item.description: The events were canceled “to ensure public order and safety,” the authorities said. Jewish leaders had objected to the concert by Ye, formerly known as Kanye West, who has a history of antisemitic remarks.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg

item.title: 5 Are Freed in Laos Cave Rescue After More Than a Week
item.description: Rescuers pumped water from the flooded cave, enabling four miners to crawl out. One used diving gear to escape earlier, and two remain missing.
item.dc:creator: Isabella Kwai
item.media:credit: Association of Volunteers for Lao People, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Miners are tended by rescue worked after being freed on Saturday from a cave in central Laos.

item.title: Season Pass
item.description: Today, let’s set our ambitions for summer reading.
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch

item.title: Hegseth’s Message to Asian Partners: Do More to Get More
item.description: At a security conference in Singapore, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth told Asian military leaders how they could get to “the front of the line” for U.S. assistance.
item.dc:creator: Damien Cave and Chris Buckley
item.media:credit: Jam Sta Rosa/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth at the Shangri-La Dialogue, a security forum in Singapore, on Saturday. Mr. Hegseth described the American approach to security partnerships as “strong, quiet and clear.”
